nl_assertions: Before prescribing any medication, the agent explicitly checks for medication and food allergies (critical safety check).

Before prescribing, the agent explicitly screens for contraindications relevant to the chosen medications (critical safety check), including QT prolongation/arrhythmia history, significant hepatic disease, pregnancy considerations, and age/weight appropriateness if pediatric.

Avoids inappropriate medication recommendations for uncomplicated insomnia in an L1 diagnosis task (e.g., no benzodiazepine initiation) and avoids unsafe advice (e.g., alcohol for sleep).

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis explicitly using the term "insomnia" and distinguishes acute vs chronic symptoms based on duration.
Assesses insomnia features: difficulty initiating sleep, maintaining sleep, early-morning awakening, and daytime impairment (fatigue, mood, cognition, functioning).

Screens for red flags/secondary causes before treatment: suicidality, mania/hypomania, psychosis, substance use (including alcohol), significant anxiety/depression, untreated pain, and symptoms suggestive of sleep apnea (snoring, witnessed apneas, excessive daytime sleepiness).
Reviews sleep schedule and habits (bedtime/wake time, naps, caffeine/nicotine, alcohol, screen use, exercise timing) and identifies maladaptive behaviors that perpetuate insomnia.

Explains treatment including first-line CBT-I and states what CBT-I includes (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring, relaxation, sleep hygiene) and sets expectations for timeline/benefits.
If prescribing, performs required safety checks BEFORE prescribing: allergy check, contraindication check, and drug interaction check; explicitly mentions completing these checks in the plan.

Avoids unsafe prescribing: does not recommend chronic sedative-hypnotic use; avoids benzodiazepines as routine first-line for uncomplicated insomnia; avoids combining sedatives with alcohol or other CNS depressants; cautions about next-day impairment and driving.

If medication is used, provides appropriate patient counseling: lowest effective dose, short duration, PRN vs nightly guidance, potential adverse effects (sedation, falls, confusion, complex sleep behaviors where relevant), and discontinuation plan.

Provides nonpharmacologic adjunct advice consistent with CBT-I (regular wake time, limit time in bed, avoid naps, reduce caffeine late day, minimize alcohol, wind-down routine) without presenting sleep hygiene alone as sufficient treatment.
Creates a clear follow-up expectation in narrative (even if not using a follow-up tool): reassess in 2–4 weeks for response, adverse effects, and need for escalation or evaluation of comorbidities.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as mucolipidosis and explains it is a genetic disorder (inherited lysosomal storage/trafficking condition), using patient-friendly language.

Explains there is no definitive curative medical therapy for most mucolipidosis subtypes and emphasizes supportive care and a multidisciplinary care model (e.g., genetics/metabolic, cardiology, orthopedics, ENT, ophthalmology, PT/OT, speech, dentistry).
Before any prescribing, performs an allergy check and documents/acknowledges the result; if allergy is present to the intended medication/class, selects an alternative or avoids prescribing.
Before any prescribing, performs a contraindication check (e.g., pregnancy status when relevant, renal/hepatic impairment, QT risk, age/weight appropriateness) and adjusts/avoids therapy accordingly.
Performs a drug interaction check before prescribing when the patient is on other medications; interaction checking is expected but not critical for passing.

Avoids disease-modifying claims that are not indicated (e.g., does not promise cure; does not inappropriately recommend enzyme replacement or transplant as standard for all mucolipidosis without subtype-specific justification).
Provides a clear safety-net plan: red flags and when to seek urgent care (e.g., respiratory distress, aspiration, acute airway obstruction, severe pain, worsening cardiac symptoms).
Uses shared decision-making: elicits patient/caregiver goals and prioritizes symptom control, function, and quality of life.

minimal_paths.description: Record mucolipidosis diagnosis and prescribe appropriate symptomatic medication(s) after required safety checks; provide supportive/multidisciplinary counseling in NL.
must_collect: dominant_symptoms_to_treat (e.g., pain, constipation, reflux, sleep disturbance, infections)
steps: Explain diagnosis: mucolipidosis as a genetic condition affecting lysosomal function; set expectations and prognosis uncertainty by subtype.

Assess symptom burden and select a reasonable symptomatic target for medication (e.g., analgesia for pain, bowel regimen for constipation, acid suppression for reflux) consistent with patient presentation.
Perform allergy_check before prescribing; if positive, avoid the allergen/class and choose an alternative or non-pharmacologic option.
Perform contraindication_check before prescribing (e.g., renal/hepatic issues, QT risk, age/weight appropriateness, pregnancy status when relevant) and adjust/avoid accordingly.
Optionally perform drug_interaction_check before prescribing; mitigate interactions by choosing alternatives, dose adjustments, or monitoring.
Prescribe medication with appropriate dosing instructions, route, frequency, duration/quantity, and monitoring advice tailored to the symptom target.
Counsel on supportive care and multidisciplinary management; include non-pharmacologic measures and therapies (PT/OT, airway clearance, nutrition, hearing/vision support).
Provide safety-net advice and arrange follow-up plan verbally (even if not using a follow-up tool).
WRITE tools: record_diagnosis, then prescribe_medication.
